label: 01 General Requirements
explainer: The GC's cost of running the job site — supervision, dumpsters, temporary fencing, port-a-potties, and most permit fees. Roughly 5-10% of total project cost. Higher on tight urban sites where staging and storage cost more.

label: 02 Existing Conditions
explainer: Tearing out what's already there — removing old finishes, hauling debris, abatement of any hazardous materials like lead paint or asbestos. Surprises live here: behind walls and under floors costs more than you'd think.

label: 03 Concrete
explainer: Foundations, slabs, footings, and any structural concrete. Higher in hillside areas (Marin, the East Bay hills) because engineered fill and seismic rebar add cost. About 8-15% of a typical ground-up build.

label: 04 Masonry
explainer: Brick, stone, block walls, fireplaces, and chimneys. Not common on modern California homes; more in older remodels or feature walls. Skilled labor — masons are pricey when you find one.

label: 05 Metals
explainer: Structural steel beams, posts, custom railings, gates. Steel prices are volatile — they swing 15-30% year-over-year with global metal markets. A line item to lock in early via your contract.

label: 06 Wood, Plastics & Composites
explainer: The wood skeleton of your house — studs, joists, rafters, sheathing — plus finish carpentry like trim, cabinets, doors. Usually 15-25% of total cost. Lumber prices have been wild since 2020; locking in early protects you.

label: 07 Thermal & Moisture Protection
explainer: Roof, siding, insulation, waterproofing, sealants. Critical for Title 24 energy compliance in CA. Spending more here pays back in energy bills for decades — cheap insulation is the single most common regret.

label: 08 Openings
explainer: All windows, exterior doors, garage doors, and skylights. Title 24 sets minimum energy ratings, so cheap big-box windows often won't pass inspection. Custom or tall windows can be 20-40% of this line.

label: 09 Finishes
explainer: Everything you actually see — drywall, paint, tile, flooring, cabinets-as-finish, countertops. The most volatile category — you can spend $30K or $300K on finishes for the same square footage.

label: 10 Specialties
explainer: Bathroom accessories, closet systems, partition walls, signage. Small-dollar line on most residential projects but easy to forget — when budgets blow, it's usually because nobody priced these.

label: 11 Equipment
explainer: Stoves, refrigerators, dishwashers, washers/dryers, and any other built-in equipment. Appliance prices have a 3-4x spread (basic vs premium). Pick early — lead times on Sub-Zero or Wolf can be 6+ months.

label: 12 Furnishings
explainer: Built-in seating, custom millwork, window treatments. Most residential budgets fold this into 06 (carpentry) instead. If it's a separate line, expect bespoke and pricey.

label: 13 Special Construction
explainer: Pre-fab buildings, swimming pools, saunas. Uncommon on most home projects. If you see this line, it usually means a pool or spa was included.

label: 14 Conveying Equipment
explainer: Elevators, dumbwaiters, accessibility lifts. Required by code for some multi-story or accessible projects. A residential elevator runs $30-80K installed, more if you're retrofitting an existing house.

label: 21 Fire Suppression
explainer: Sprinkler systems. California requires residential sprinklers on most new single-family homes built since 2011 — typically $1.50-$3.00 per square foot.

label: 22 Plumbing
explainer: Water supply lines, drain/waste/vent, gas lines, fixtures, water heater. About 5-10% of total cost. Tankless water heaters and water filtration are common upgrades worth pricing here.

label: 23 HVAC
explainer: Furnace, AC, ductwork, heat pumps, mini-splits, ventilation. Title 24 keeps pushing toward heat pumps. About 5-10% of total — higher when your existing system needs full replacement and not just an extension.

label: 26 Electrical
explainer: Panel, wiring, outlets, switches, lighting, EV chargers, solar tie-in. About 6-10% of total. If you're adding load (EVs, induction cooking, heat pumps), you may need a 200A or 400A service upgrade.

label: 27 Communications
explainer: Structured wiring for internet, TV, security, smart-home. Run conduit during framing or live with wireless forever — retrofit pulls cost 3-5x more than installing during the rough-in phase.

label: 28 Electronic Safety & Security
explainer: Burglar alarms, security cameras, smoke and CO detectors. Code requires hardwired interconnected smoke alarms — DIY battery-only doesn't pass inspection.

label: 31 Earthwork
explainer: Digging, hauling dirt, retaining walls, soil compaction. Massively variable — flat lots are cheap, hillside lots can run 5-10x more. Always get a geotech report before pricing this on a sloped or filled lot.

label: 32 Exterior Improvements
explainer: Driveways, patios, fences, walkways, landscape irrigation. Often pushed off the main contract and bid separately to landscape contractors. Budget 5-15% of building cost depending on lot size.

label: 33 Utilities
explainer: Connecting to (or running new) water, sewer, gas, and electrical service lines from the street to your house. Big variable — a 200ft run from the street can add $30-100K, especially if you need a sewer ejector or trenching through pavement.
